content:
Revit’s Toposolid (introduced in 2024, replacing the older Toposurface for most workflows) can be built from a CSV of elevation points. The workflow is straightforward once your CSV is formatted correctly, but a few small mistakes in the file will silently break the import.
This post walks through the CSV format Revit expects, the exact steps to import it, and the things that trip people up.
What the CSV needs to look like Revit expects each row to be one point, in the form:
X, Y, ZRules that matter:
Comma separated. Semicolons and tabs will not work. If you’re in a locale where Excel writes semicolons by default (Germany, France, several others), export with a saved CSV format or open the file and swap them for commas before importing. No header row. Revit reads row 1 as the first point. If you have X,Y,Z at the top, Revit tries to import it as a point and either fails or produces a stray vertex at (0,0,0). Dot as decimal separator. Use 123.45, not 123,45. Same locale issue as above. Consistent units. Revit imports the numbers as-is in the units you specify at import. If your project is in metres, the CSV should be metres. If the CSV is in feet and the project is in metres, everything comes in about 3.28 times too small. Reasonable coordinate magnitudes. Revit warns you when points are more than about 20 miles (32 km) from the internal origin. This is not just a warning, geometry precision genuinely degrades that far out. If your XYZ values are full UTM coordinates, subtract a fixed offset before writing the CSV and record the offset for later shared-coordinates setup. Step by step: CSV to Toposolid Open your Revit project. Make sure a site plan view is active. Go to Massing & Site → Toposolid → Create from Import. Choose the CSV / points file option. Browse to your CSV. Set the units in the dialog to match the CSV contents (feet or metres). Confirm. Revit reads the points, triangulates them, and places the Toposolid on the site.
Point density: how many is enough More points is not always better. A dense CSV (tens of thousands of points from LiDAR) produces a very heavy Toposolid, which slows Revit down and does not render meaningfully better than a moderate-density surface.
Practical guidance:
Small residential site (up to about half an acre): 500 to 2,000 points is plenty. Medium commercial site (a few acres): 2,000 to 8,000 points. Large master plan or infrastructure site: 8,000 to 20,000 points, and consider splitting into multiple Toposolids. If your source data is denser than this, thin it before import. Terrain tools like Topo-grapher let you set an output resolution so you do not have to trim manually.
Coordinate systems and shared coordinates If you plan to overlay the Toposolid with other georeferenced data (aerial imagery, GIS layers, infrastructure design), set up shared coordinates before you import.
Set your project’s Survey Point to the true site coordinates. Subtract the survey point coordinates from your CSV before export, so the imported points sit near the Revit internal origin. After import, the Toposolid reads correctly in shared coordinates while staying near the internal origin geometrically. Doing this in the other order (importing full UTM values first, then trying to shift the project) is possible but painful. Set up survey point first.
Common errors and fixes “The imported file is not valid.” Nearly always a formatting issue: semicolons instead of commas, an extra header row, or non-numeric values in a row.
Toposolid comes in flat at Z=0. The Z column is being read as text. Check the CSV for stray quotes around numbers.
Toposolid is the wrong scale. Unit mismatch between the CSV and the Revit project. Re-check the import dialog units.
Toposolid has spikes or holes. Duplicate points at the same XY with different Z values create spikes. Points too far apart create holes. Both are fixable by cleaning the CSV before import.
Where the CSV comes from You can generate an XYZ CSV from raw survey data, LiDAR, or public elevation datasets. The public datasets (USGS 3DEP, ArcticDEM, Ordnance Survey, national mapping agencies across Europe) are freely licensed for commercial work but come as GeoTIFFs, not CSVs. You have to convert them yourself, or use a tool that does.
Topo-grapher does that conversion in the browser. Pick a site on the map, choose XYZ CSV as the output, and you get a Revit-ready file with the right coordinate handling for your region: geoid correction on the vertical, matching CRS, adjustable point density.
summary: Format your CSV correctly, import it into Revit 2024+, and get a clean Toposolid without the usual coordinate and unit headaches.
title: How to Create a Toposolid in Revit From a CSV of Elevation Points

content:
Importing a site surface into ArchiCAD sounds simple until you try it with real survey data. The Mesh tool accepts a few different inputs, and which one you pick affects mesh density, coordinate accuracy, and how much cleanup you do afterwards.
This post walks through the three import paths ArchiCAD supports for site terrain and when to use each.
What ArchiCAD needs from your terrain data The native terrain object in ArchiCAD is the Mesh. It’s a triangulated surface with per-vertex heights, used for placing the building, cut-fill calculations, and renderings.
You can build a Mesh from:
A CSV or TXT file of XYZ points (surveyor’s data)
A DXF or DWG with 3D polylines (contours) or 3D points
An IFC file containing an IfcSite element with terrain geometry
All three work. The one you pick usually comes down to what your data provider gives you.
Method 1: XYZ points from a CSV file This is the cleanest path if you have raw elevation data.
Prepare your CSV so each row is X, Y, Z with no header row.
Make sure the units match your ArchiCAD project (usually metres for European projects, feet for US).
In ArchiCAD, go to File → Interoperability → Mesh → Place Mesh from Surveyor’s Data.
Point it at your CSV.
ArchiCAD triangulates the points and drops the Mesh at the origin (or offset, depending on your input coordinates).
Watch out for very large coordinates. If your survey uses full UTM or national grid values (say, six-digit northings), ArchiCAD’s precision suffers when you’re that far from origin. The usual fix is to offset the coordinates so the site sits closer to 0,0 in the CSV, and record the true offset separately for later re-registration.
Method 2: DXF contours If your data comes as contour lines instead of points, DXF is the format most CAD tools export cleanly.
Open a fresh worksheet in ArchiCAD.
Go to File → External Content → Merge, then select the DXF.
Set the translator to preserve 3D data. Default translators sometimes flatten Z.
Once the contours are visible, place a Mesh over the area and use Adjust Mesh to Slabs / Surfaces to lift the mesh vertices to the contour elevations.
DXF is slower than direct CSV import and needs more cleanup, but it’s often the format you get from local government terrain services. Contours also give you softer transitions than raw points if the point density is low.
Method 3: IFC site element If someone else in the project has already modelled the terrain in another BIM tool (Revit, Rhino, whatever), ask them for an IFC export.
Go to File → Interoperability → IFC → Merge IFC Model.
Choose an IFC translator with IfcSite enabled.
The imported site element comes in as a Morph or Mesh depending on how the sender exported it.
This is the path if you’re joining a project mid-flight and someone else owns the survey. It skips the format conversion step, but the geometry quality depends entirely on how it was authored on the other side.
Which format gives the best result For accuracy: XYZ CSV, straight into the Mesh from Surveyor’s Data workflow. No lossy conversion, no over-simplified contours, direct control over point density.
For speed: IFC, if someone else has already done the modelling.
For legacy data: DXF, when contours are all you can get.
Getting the elevation data in the first place The tricky part is usually getting a clean XYZ file that matches your project extents and coordinate system. Government elevation data is available for most countries, but it comes in raster formats (GeoTIFF, ASC) that you have to convert to points first, and you need to know the geoid model and CRS to get vertical accuracy right.
Topo-grapher handles that conversion in the browser. Pick a site on the map, choose your output format, and you get a CSV of XYZ points, a DXF with contours, or an IFC file with an IfcSite element. All three ArchiCAD import paths, from the same source data, in your project’s coordinate system.
No GIS setup, no format juggling, no wondering whether the heights are ellipsoidal or orthometric.
summary: Three practical ways to bring accurate site terrain into ArchiCAD, plus which format gives the cleanest mesh.
title: How to Import a Site Surface into ArchiCAD

content:
So you’re trying to get real site terrain into Rhino. Good news: it’s not complicated once you know the two routes. Let me walk you through both.
The problem most people hit first
You’ve got a site. You need topography. Rhino doesn’t pull elevation data from anywhere natively, so you’re either tracing contours off a PDF survey or wrestling with mesh files that don’t match your actual site. Neither is a good use of your time.
Route 1: XYZ point cloud to mesh
Export an XYZ CSV from Topo-grapher, draw your site boundary on the map, and download. You get a grid of elevation points covering your exact site. In Rhino, go to File > Import, select your CSV, and Rhino brings it in as a point cloud. From there, run Patch or Drape to build a surface, or use QuadRemesh if you want a clean mesh for rendering. If you’re in Grasshopper, even simpler: feed the XYZ points into a Delaunay mesh component and you’ve got a structured terrain surface in about 10 nodes.
Route 2: DXF contours
Topo-grapher also exports DXF contour lines at adaptive intervals based on your site’s relief. Import the DXF directly into Rhino, then loft or patch between the contours to build your surface. Good option if you prefer working from lines rather than points.
Which one to use
Point cloud if you want speed and you’re heading into Grasshopper. DXF if you want contour lines you can adjust before building the surface. Either way, you’re starting from real government LiDAR data, not a traced PDF. Go draw your site boundary at topo-grapher.com and download your file. Takes about 30 seconds.
summary:
So you’re trying to get real site terrain into Rhino. Good news: it’s not complicated once you know the two routes. Let me walk you through both.

The problem most people hit first

You’ve got a site. You need topography. Rhino doesn’t pull elevation data from anywhere natively, so you’re either tracing contours off a PDF survey or wrestling with mesh files that don’t match your actual site. Neither is a good use of your time.
title: Site terrain Rhino

content:
Importing site terrain into SketchUp from real survey data might be one of the most important things an architect can learn, whether you’re still a student or ten years into the job. Most of you will disagree with that, which I completely understand. And to be fair, the disagreement is reasonable, because it isn’t the importing itself that matters, and it isn’t your SketchUp skills either. Software can be learned. Talent and an eye for things cannot. Those get carved out slowly, through experience, and curated carefully over years.
So why call importing terrain an important skill at all? Because the real skill is knowing where to spend your time. It’s using your hours on design, where they actually count, instead of bleeding them into modelling and redrawing terrain by hand. Pulling real survey-grade data into your model isn’t a skill in itself. It’s a way to get your time back, so you’re not losing hours (sometimes days) building a terrain that already exists as data. Use your talent where it matters. Don’t waste it tracing contours.
Get your terrain from Topo-grapher instead, because I know that pain firsthand, and that’s exactly why I built the tool.
My name is Aki, and I’m an Architect too. I could not stand to lose any more of my time drawing terrain that should have come from real data instead of guesswork. So I made something that gets you real terrain in seconds, ready to drop into your software. All you have to do is check it against your survey documents, fix whatever needs fixing, and move on. No starting from point zero.
Ready to stop drawing terrain by hand? Generate your site terrain in seconds at Topo-grapher.
summary: Importing site terrain into SketchUp from real survey data might be one of the most important things an architect can learn, whether you’re still a student or ten years into the job. Most of you will disagree with that, which I completely understand. And to be fair, the disagreement is reasonable, because it isn’t the importing itself that matters, and it isn’t your SketchUp skills either. Software can be learned. Talent and an eye for things cannot. Those get carved out slowly, through experience, and curated carefully over years.
title: Import real terrain into sketchup

content:
If you’ve ever tried to build accurate terrain in Revit, SketchUp or Rhino by hand, you already know the feeling. It’s slow, it’s fiddly, and somehow the ground never quite sits where the survey says it should.
Here in Iceland it’s a bit of a ritual. For most sites you can dig up a survey document, on paper, with the elevation points marked. Then you get to redraw the whole site and its levels by hand, square by square, hoping the result is close enough to reality to design against. Spoiler: it usually isn’t, not as close as you’d actually want.
So I built a tool to do the boring part for me. It pulls real elevations from local and national surveys and builds the site for you, right inside the software you already use.
The toposolid bit is the hard bit Plenty of tools can spit out terrain. The part nobody seems to get right is handing you a toposolid you can actually work with natively in Revit, not a frozen IFC mesh you have to fight every time the design moves.
That’s the difference with topo-grapher. You can import your terrain as a real toposolid instead of an IFC, which means you can adjust the landscape as the project changes, push it to the elevation you need, and have it behave properly inside your normal workflow. No re-linking, no “why won’t this surface edit,” no quietly rebuilding it by hand anyway.
One honest caveat I’ll always tell you to check the result against your surveyor or whatever site survey you already have. It’s good data, but it’s your project and your liability, so trust, but verify.
That said, this saves a genuinely silly number of hours on every project. Hours you can spend designing instead of tracing contour lines, plus the money it quietly saves the whole team.
Give it a go at topo-grapher.com, and ask away if you have any questions.
summary:
If you’ve ever tried to build accurate terrain in Revit, SketchUp or Rhino by hand, you already know the feeling. It’s slow, it’s fiddly, and somehow the ground never quite sits where the survey says it should.

Here in Iceland it’s a bit of a ritual. For most sites you can dig up a survey document, on paper, with the elevation points marked. Then you get to redraw the whole site and its levels by hand, square by square, hoping the result is close enough to reality to design against. Spoiler: it usually isn’t, not as close as you’d actually want.
title: How to Import a Toposolid into Revit (Without the Headache)
